prompt:
LOS ANGELES — The latest crash involving a Tesla in Autopilot mode didn’t turn tragic, as some past ones have, but certainly was embarrassing.
A Tesla Model S veered into a parked police cruiser Tuesday, severely damaging both vehicles in Laguna Beach, Calif., a coastal community south of Los Angeles.
The driver, a 65-year-old from Laguna Niguel, Calif., told officers that he had engaged the car’s partial self-driving system, called Autopilot. “He told us in his own statement he was in driver-assisted mode,” police Sgt. Jim Cota said.
The driver suffered minor injuries, Cota said. The parked cruiser was unoccupied, the officer standing about 100 feet away off Laguna Canyon Road as he responded to a call.
Cota said the luxury electric car crashed in almost the same place as another Tesla about a year ago. The driver, he said, also pointed to the Autopilot system as being engaged.
The crash comes as Tesla has been facing scrutiny involving Autopilot. Since March, a driver died when his Model X SUV hit a safety barrier in Mountain View, Calif., while in Autopilot mode. And another driver in Salt Lake City was injured in a crash when her car hit a parked fire truck.
Regarding the latest crash, Tesla issued a statement, saying: "When using Autopilot, drivers are continuously reminded of their responsibility to keep their hands on the wheel and maintain control of the vehicle at all times."
The statement also said that Tesla "has always been clear that Autopilot doesn’t make the car impervious to all accidents, and before a driver can use Autopilot, they must accept a dialogue box which states that ‘Autopilot is designed for use on highways that have a center divider and clear lane markings.'"
Can you summarize the recent Tesla crash in Autopilot mode and the company's response to it?
response: A Tesla Model S in Autopilot mode crashed into a parked police cruiser in Laguna Beach, California, causing extensive damage, while the police car was empty. The driver, a 65-year-old resident, suffered minor injuries, later telling police he had the partial self-driving system activated. Tesla has reminded drivers to keep their hands on the wheel while using Autopilot, and issued a statement saying the driver had signed a dialogue box confirming the system was designed for use only on highways that had a central divider and clear lane markings.

prompt: Write a vivid and suspenseful story about a diverse group of survivors who have to navigate a bleak post-apocalyptic world filled with danger at every turn. Describe the brutal conditions they face, the relentless foes they encounter, and the harrowing obstacles they must overcome to stay alive. Make sure to develop each character's unique personality, motivations, and strengths as they work together to survive in this unforgiving landscape. Will they be able to find a way to safety, or will the dangers that surround them prove to be too much to overcome? Let your imagination run wild as you craft a gripping tale of survival and resilience amidst the ashes of civilization.
response:
The world as we knew it had come to an end. The virus that swept across the globe had wiped out nearly all of humanity, leaving behind a barren and unforgiving landscape where only the strongest could survive. In this new world, there were no more governments, no more laws, and no more police to keep the peace. Instead, there were only those who were willing to do whatever it took to survive.

Among the survivors were a diverse group of people, each with their own unique personalities, strengths, and motivations. There was Jake, a former soldier who had seen too much death and destruction in his lifetime; Emma, a young college student who had been studying biology before the world had collapsed; Greg, a mechanic who had always been handy with fixing things; and Marcus, a tough-as-nails ex-con who had survived in prison for years. Despite their differences, they all had one thing in common: the will to survive.

Their journey began in a desolate city, where they scrounged for food and supplies in abandoned stores and homes. Every step they took was fraught with danger, as they never knew what might be lurking around the next corner. Whether it was feral dogs, infected humans, or other hostile survivors, they had to be ready for anything at all times.

One day, they stumbled upon a group of survivors hiding out in a fortified building. The group was led by a man named Michael, who seemed to have everything under control. They were growing food, had a reliable source of water, and even had a doctor to tend to any injuries. At first, the new group seemed like a godsend, but soon Jake began to suspect that Michael was hiding something.

His suspicions were confirmed when he discovered that Michael was hoarding supplies, taking more than his fair share and leaving the rest of the group to fend for themselves. When Jake confronted him, Michael became defensive and threatened to kick the group out if they didn't fall in line.

With their options limited, Jake and the others decided to leave the safety of the fortified building and strike out on their own once again. They traveled for days, facing harsh weather conditions and running low on supplies. Just when it seemed like all hope was lost, they stumbled upon a hidden underground bunker that was well-stocked with food and water.

But as they settled into their new surroundings, they soon realized that they were not alone. Another group had also found the bunker and they were not willing to share. It was a brutal fight for survival, but in the end, Jake and the others emerged victorious.

As they sat in the safety of the bunker, the reality of their situation set in. There was no guarantee of safety in this post-apocalyptic world, no guarantee that they would ever find a way to safety. But they had each other, and that was enough to give them hope for the future. Together, they would continue to fight and survive, no matter what challenges lay ahead.

prompt:
Given the text: Form + Structure: Christian allegory of temptation, fall, and Redemption. Rossetti does this to challenge the decidedly patriarchal perception of norms within Victorian culture in terms of sexuality to reconstruct the Christian idea of redemption.
“cottage maiden/ Hardened by sum and air” – the use of imagery depicts a labourers life, imagery of dirt, shows the distinction of social class will be prominent in the narrative.
The fact that the narrator was “lured” makes it sound as if she was an innocent “maiden”. The verb “lured” conveys Thomas’s predatory nature.
“Contented... Cottage” – the hard alliteration of the letter ‘C’, conveys a bitter and edgier tone.
“shameless shamefull”- the Oxymoron/Sibilance reflects the hushed manner in which the speaker was ensnared by the “great lord”, she was taken in then cast aside like “an unclean thing”.
“changed me like a glove” – the simile objectifies her, moreover by describing herself as a glove, the speaker acknowledges that she lost sight of her own needs and desires in an attempt to please and suit the lord. “O Lady Kate, my cousin Kate” – the narrator employs a tome of sarcasm and condescension. The speaker tries a faltering attempt to belittle her cousin; however it just ends up highlighting her jealousy/ bitterness.
How does Rossetti challenge Victorian societal norms of female sexuality in "Cousin Kate"? Provide specific examples from the text.
response: Rossetti challenges Victorian societal norms of female sexuality in "Cousin Kate" by portraying the female protagonist as a victim of societal expectations and norms. The use of Christian allegory of temptation, fall, and redemption challenges the idea that women who engage in sexual activities before marriage are impure and unredeemable. The imagery of the "cottage maiden" and the distinction of social class highlights the unequal distribution of power in Victorian society, where women of lower social classes were often exploited. The fact that the female protagonist was "lured" and referred to as an innocent "maiden" challenges the idea that women are responsible for their own seduction or downfall. The use of the oxymoron/sibilance "shameless shamefull" reflects the hushed manner in which women's sexuality was discussed in Victorian society. The simile "changed me like a glove" objectifies the female protagonist and highlights the idea that women were expected to conform to the desires of men. The use of sarcasm and condescension towards the end of the poem challenges the idea that women who engage in premarital sexual activities are jealous and bitter. Overall, Rossetti challenges Victorian societal norms of female sexuality by portraying the female protagonist as a victim of societal expectations and norms.

prompt:
The Migodo (singular Ng´godo)of the Chopi people of southern Mozambique are among the most exciting spectacles to be witnessed in Africa. They are annually staged entertainments made up of dances, songs and music played on orchestras of massed xylophones played by up to 48 musicians.
In the 1940s, when the musicologist Hugh Tracey made the recordings posted here, the orchestras were to be found in every large village. Katini weNyambombe was the leader and composer at the court of the Paramount Chief Wani Zavala, and Gomukomo weSimbi held the same office at the court of Filippe we Madumane Banguza whose district, called Mangene, lies along the north-western bounday of Zavala.
Each Ng’godo has certain set features, most prominently the orchestral introductions and finale, the Mdano or Call to the Dancers, and the Mzeno, the principal Song. The best of them have a set theme.
In Katini’s Ng’godo for 1940, the theme is Portuguese colonial rule, interspersed with very local satire. It is made up of the following sections, the whole performance lasting 50 minutes.

Movements 1,2, 3 and 11 have no words to them.

The charge is that the Portuguese consume everything, eggs and chickens, without regard for the future.
Chopi migrants to the South African mines were paid in English pounds. They resented having to change these to Portuguese escudos on their return, at discounts of up to 10%.
Kapitini: a newly-appointed court messenger, throwing his weight around.
Bakubakwane: an official serving Chief Wani Zavala, had brought a message to Katini he was to finish building his hut before the rains. Kapitini then countermanded this order, telling Katini to work on his Timbila, and sending his men, Malova and Dibuliani, to beat him up, along with his wife Mashewani who died shortly afterwards.
Sjambok: A whip, made of hippo or rhino hide.
Chipau and Fainde are fellow musicians, called as witnesses.
Not the pound of the 4th movement, but the colonial hut tax.
The reference is to the palmatoria, a perforated wooden paddle used for corporal punishment in Portuguese schools and in the colonies.
Sibuyeye was not a Chopi by birth, but related to the Gaza (Shangaans), across the Limpopo River. His appointment as a chief’s messenger was resented, especially when he intruded into Mahashi Bingwana, closed to Katini’s home village.
A man called Mahambane had reported to Wani Zavala that Katini was neglecting his music. In fact, he was in his hut composing his ng’godo, and Faine his fellow musician is called as witness.
Chimuke was the nickname for an earlier Portuguese chefe, and a stickler for etiquette.
A slightly complicated reference to a dispute over a chieftaincy, which was resolved as the next four lines describe.
Another chieftaincy dispute, which was settled in Manjengwe’s favour after Fambanyane had resorted to violence.
Masawa: Katini’s sister in law, an attractive widow.
Her admirer who was slow in proposing. The song teases Chidodo into making up his mind.
Can you provide a summary of the Ng'godo performances by the Chopi people of Mozambique, including the set features and themes?
response: The Ng'godo performances are annual entertainments made up of dances, songs, and music played on massed xylophones by up to 48 musicians, performed by the Chopi people of southern Mozambique. The orchestral introductions and finales, the Mdano or Call to the Dancers, and the Mzeno, the principal song, are the set features of each Ng'godo. The best of them have a set theme, and in Katini's Ng'godo for 1940, the theme is Portuguese colonial rule, interspersed with local satire. The performance lasts for 50 minutes and is made up of 11 movements. The Ng'godo has been recorded by the musicologist Hugh Tracey in the 1940s, and each movement has its backstory and meaning.
